questions.stem:
The products A and B in the following reactions, respectively are:

$\text{A} \xleftarrow{\text{Ag-NO}_2} \ce{CH3-CH2-CH2-Br} \xrightarrow{\text{AgCN}} \text{B}$
questions.explanation:
**Reaction with $\ce{AgNO2}$:**

$\ce{AgNO2}$ is an ionic silver salt. The $\ce{NO2^-}$ ion is an **ambident nucleophile** (can attack through N or O).

With $\ce{AgNO2}$: The silver coordinates with oxygen, making O the attacking end → **alkyl nitrite** ($\ce{R-O-N=O}$) is the major product.

$\ce{CH3CH2CH2Br + AgNO2 -> CH3CH2CH2-ONO}$ (propyl nitrite) → **A = $\ce{CH3CH2CH2-ONO}$**

**Reaction with AgCN:**

$\ce{AgCN}$ is a covalent silver compound. The $\ce{CN^-}$ is an ambident nucleophile (attacks through C or N).

With $\ce{AgCN}$: Silver coordinates with N, making C the attacking end → **nitrile** ($\ce{R-CN}$) is the major product.

$\ce{CH3CH2CH2Br + AgCN -> CH3CH2CH2-CN}$ (butanenitrile) → **B = $\ce{CH3CH2CH2-CN}$**

**Answer: A = $\ce{CH3CH2CH2-ONO}$, B = $\ce{CH3CH2CH2-CN}$ → Option (b)**

**Key Points:**
- $\ce{AgNO2}$ (ionic) → O-attack → alkyl nitrite ($\ce{R-ONO}$)
- $\ce{AgCN}$ (covalent) → C-attack → nitrile ($\ce{R-CN}$)
- $\ce{NaNO2}$ (ionic) → N-attack → nitroalkane ($\ce{R-NO2}$)
- $\ce{KCN}$ (ionic) → C-attack → nitrile ($\ce{R-CN}$)

questions.stem: Propane molecule on chlorination under photochemical condition gives two di-chloro products, "x" and "y". Amongst "x" and "y", "x" is an optically active molecule. How many tri-chloro products (consider only structural isomers) will be obtained from "x" when it is further treated with chlorine under the photochemical condition?
questions.explanation:
**🧠 First Find Which Dichloride Is Chiral — That Tells You Where to Start**

Read this question carefully. Propane is chlorinated under light, giving two dichloro isomers. One of them, "x", is optically active. We then have to chlorinate "x" again and count how many tri-chloro **structural** isomers come out (the question says "structural", so ignore stereoisomers in the second step).

The first thing to nail down: which dichloropropane is chiral?

Propane is $\ce{CH3-CH2-CH3}$. The possible dichlorides are:
- $\ce{CH3-CHCl-CH2Cl}$ (1,2-dichloropropane)
- $\ce{CHCl2-CH2-CH3}$ (1,1-dichloropropane)
- $\ce{CH3-CCl2-CH3}$ (2,2-dichloropropane)
- $\ce{ClCH2-CH2-CH2Cl}$ (1,3-dichloropropane)

Check each for a chiral centre. In **1,2-dichloropropane**, $\ce{CH3-CHCl-CH2Cl}$, the central C has $\ce{H}$, $\ce{Cl}$, $\ce{CH3}$, $\ce{CH2Cl}$ — four different groups $\rightarrow$ **chiral**. The others have either no chiral centre or symmetry that kills it. So **$\ce{x} = \ce{CH3-CHCl-CH2Cl}$** (1,2-dichloropropane).

**🗺️ Working It Out**
![image](https://pub-2ff04ffcdd1247b6b8d19c44c1dfe553.r2.dev/shared/image/1779627647915_Screenshot_2026-05-24_at_6.16.52_PM_7bead4cf.webp)

**⚡ The Smart Move**

For "how many products from chlorination" problems, walk through three steps every time: (1) draw the substrate clearly, (2) mark each distinct H environment by symmetry, (3) replace one H at a time to count products. If the question says "structural isomers only", you can ignore chirality at the new $\ce{Cl}$-bearing carbon. If it says "consider all isomers", count stereoisomers too.

**⚠️ Where Students Get Stuck**

Two common mistakes. **One:** picking the wrong dichloride for "x". Students sometimes pick $\ce{CHCl2-CH2-CH3}$ (1,1-) because it "looks asymmetric" — but the $\ce{CCl2}$ carbon has two Cls (identical), so it is not chiral. Always check that all four substituents on a carbon are **truly different** before calling it a chiral centre. **Two:** missing the C3 ($\ce{-CH3}$) products. Students sometimes focus only on the carbons that already have Cl and forget that the methyl end can also be chlorinated.

$\boxed{\text{Answer: (a) } 3 \text{ structural tri-chloro isomers}}$

questions.stem:
Predict the major product of the following reaction sequence:
![image](https://pub-2ff04ffcdd1247b6b8d19c44c1dfe553.r2.dev/questions/a26aa9f3-ea33-46cc-806a-24111d64dd6b/svg/1772175904167_Chem_20260226_135620_525338000_fca9bed6.svg)
questions.explanation:
**🧠 Three-Step Sequence — Track What Each Step Does Before Picking the Final Product**

Read the question carefully. The substrate is **methylcyclohexane** and we run three steps. Each step changes one thing — write out the intermediate after each step before deciding the final product.

The three reagents:
- (1) $\ce{Br2}/\text{h}\nu$ — radical bromination, goes to most stable C-H ($3^\circ$ here).
- (2) Alcoholic KOH, $\Delta$ — E2 elimination, gives the **Zaitsev** (more substituted) alkene.
- (3) $\ce{HBr}/\text{ROOR}$ ($\text{peroxide}$), h$\nu$ — **anti-Markovnikov** HBr addition via radicals.

**🖼️ Reactions**
![image](https://pub-2ff04ffcdd1247b6b8d19c44c1dfe553.r2.dev/shared/image/1779629552041_Screenshot_2026-05-24_at_6.17.51_PM_6f85acfa.webp)

**⚠️ Where Students Get Stuck**

Two common mistakes. **One:** forgetting the peroxide effect and ending up at option (a) (the Markovnikov product). The peroxide-radical mechanism is restricted to $\ce{HBr}$ — it does NOT work for $\ce{HCl}$ or $\ce{HI}$ (the Br radical step is uniquely thermodynamically and kinetically suited). Even so, if peroxide is written, flip the regiochemistry. **Two:** picking option (d) (the 7-membered ring), which would require a ring expansion that does not happen here — the substrate has no carbocation step to drive Wagner-Meerwein.

$\boxed{\text{Answer: (b) — 1-methyl-2-bromocyclohexane (anti-Markovnikov)}}$

questions.stem:
In the following series of reactions identify the major products A & B respectively.
![image](https://pub-2ff04ffcdd1247b6b8d19c44c1dfe553.r2.dev/questions/318d021f-f18e-4152-b0aa-4b5d2e1ba571/svg/1772176002123_Chem_20260226_135643_005896000_951f1a28.svg)
questions.explanation:
**🧠 Two Directing Effects, One Position — Find the Carbon Where Both Groups Agree**

Read the question carefully. We sulfonate bromobenzene, then brominate the product. Two electrophilic aromatic substitutions in a row. The trick is to think about **directing effects** at each step.

For the first step ($\ce{SO3}/\ce{H2SO4}$ on bromobenzene): the $\ce{Br}$ group is **ortho/para directing** (lone pair donates by resonance, even though it is weakly electron-withdrawing by induction). With a small electrophile like $\ce{SO3}$, the para position is sterically favoured. So **A = $p$-bromobenzenesulfonic acid**.

For the second step ($\ce{Br2}/\ce{Fe}$ on A): now the ring carries two directors. The original $\ce{Br}$ at C4 directs to C3, C5 (ortho positions) and C1 (para, blocked by $\ce{SO3H}$). The $\ce{SO3H}$ at C1 is **meta directing** (strongly electron-withdrawing by resonance), pointing to C3 and C5. Both directors push toward the **same positions** — C3 (or equivalently C5). The new $\ce{Br}$ lands at C3.
![image](https://pub-2ff04ffcdd1247b6b8d19c44c1dfe553.r2.dev/shared/image/1779629703003_Screenshot_2026-05-24_at_6.17.58_PM_f7075ef2.webp)

$\boxed{\text{Answer: (b) — A: } p\text{-Br-C}_6\text{H}_4\text{-SO}_3\text{H; B: 3,4-Br}_2\text{-C}_6\text{H}_3\text{-SO}_3\text{H}}$

questions.stem: An optically active alkyl halide $\ce{C4H9Br}$ [A] reacts with hot KOH dissolved in ethanol and forms product which reacts with bromine to give dibromide [C]. The compound [C] is converted into a gas [D] upon treatment with alcoholic KOH. During hydration 18 gram of water is added to 1 mole of gas [D] on warming with mercuric sulphate and dilute acid at 333 K to form compound [E]. The IUPAC name of compound [E] is:
questions.explanation:
**🧠 Walk Through Each Step, Track the Functional Group**

Read this multi-step problem step by step. We are given A (an optically active $\ce{C4H9Br}$) and have to find E (the final hydration product). The method: identify A first, then follow each reagent one at a time and note what the functional group becomes. Optically active $\ce{C4H9Br}$ must have a chiral centre. The only $\ce{C4H9Br}$ with a chiral C is **2-bromobutane** ($\ce{CH3-CH2-CHBr-CH3}$, with four different groups on C2). Start from there.
![image](https://pub-2ff04ffcdd1247b6b8d19c44c1dfe553.r2.dev/shared/image/1779629963698_Screenshot_2026-05-24_at_6.18.10_PM_768f885a.webp)



**⚡ The Smart Move**

For multi-step sequences, write the structure of each intermediate before moving on. Three habits to build:
1. "Optically active" + alkyl halide $\rightarrow$ chiral $\alpha$-C $\rightarrow$ likely secondary position.
2. **Alc. KOH** $\rightarrow$ E2 elimination (Zaitsev's most-substituted alkene).
3. **Alkyne + $\ce{H2O}/\ce{Hg^{2+}}$** $\rightarrow$ Markovnikov hydration; the enol tautomerises to a **ketone** (for internal alkynes) or a **methyl ketone** (for terminal alkynes). Never an aldehyde, except from acetylene itself.

**⚠️ Where Students Get Stuck**

Two common traps. **One:** picking option (d) butan-1-al (an aldehyde). Internal alkynes never give aldehydes on hydration — only ketones. Aldehydes come only from acetylene ($\ce{HC\#CH}$) hydration. **Two:** missing the second E2 step (C $\rightarrow$ D). Two adjacent halogens require two equivalents of base; if you stop at the first elimination, you end up at a vinyl bromide instead of the alkyne. Alc. KOH is strong and abundant enough to do both eliminations.

$\boxed{\text{Answer: (c) — Butan-2-one}}$

questions.stem:
![image](https://pub-2ff04ffcdd1247b6b8d19c44c1dfe553.r2.dev/questions/6fe9fd88-f526-4a27-94b8-c25b47508a93/svg/1772176232935_Chem_20260226_140010_041955000_d64a1ed8.svg)
The maximum number of RBr producing 2-Methylbutane by above sequence of reactions is __________. (Consider the structural isomers only)
questions.explanation:
**🧠 Count the Unique Carbons of 2-Methylbutane — Each One Gives One RBr Precursor**

Read the question slowly. RBr + Mg/dry ether $\rightarrow$ RMgBr (Grignard), then $\ce{H2O}$ protonates the carbanion to give R-H. So the net reaction is "Br on a carbon gets replaced by H". To get 2-methylbutane as the final R-H, RBr is just 2-methylbutane with Br swapped for an H at one position.

The question becomes: **how many distinct carbons in 2-methylbutane have at least one H** (so we can put Br there)? Each distinct position gives one unique RBr.

**🖼️ Reading the Structure**

2-methylbutane: $\ce{CH3-CH(CH3)-CH2-CH3}$. Label the carbons:
- C1: the terminal methyl on the branched side ($\ce{CH3-}$, attached to C2)
- C2: the branched carbon ($\ce{-CH(CH3)-}$), bearing one H + the branch methyl
- C2': the branch methyl on C2 (equivalent to C1 by symmetry — both are methyls attached to C2)
- C3: the methylene ($\ce{-CH2-}$)
- C4: the other terminal methyl ($\ce{-CH3}$, attached to C3)

**🗺️ Working It Out**

By symmetry, **C1 and C2' (the branch methyl) are equivalent** — both are primary methyls hanging off C2. So count them as one position. C4 is a different primary methyl (attached to C3, which is attached to C2). C4 is **not** equivalent to C1 because their distances from the branch are different.

So the unique positions are:
1. C1 / C2' (the two equivalent branch methyls — primary)
2. C2 (the tertiary branch carbon — has 1 H)
3. C3 (the methylene — secondary, has 2 H)
4. C4 (the chain-end methyl — primary, different environment from C1)

That gives **4 unique RBr precursors**:
- 1-bromo-2-methylbutane (Br on C1 or equivalently C2')
- 2-bromo-2-methylbutane (Br on C2 — tertiary halide)
- 2-bromo-3-methylbutane (Br on C3 — secondary halide)
- 1-bromo-3-methylbutane (Br on C4 — primary, far from branch)

All four can form a Grignard with Mg (even the tertiary, though it is less stable) and all four give 2-methylbutane on aqueous workup. Maximum = **4**.

**⚡ The Smart Move**

For Grignard "how many precursors give X" questions, count **distinct H-bearing carbons in X** (by molecular symmetry, not just by carbon count). Two methyls attached to the same carbon are equivalent; two methyls attached to different carbons in different environments are not.

Two more rules to lock in: Grignard reagents from $1^\circ$, $2^\circ$, and $3^\circ$ alkyl halides all form, and all protonate cleanly with water. Vinylic and aryl halides ($\ce{Ar-X}$, $\ce{CH2=CH-X}$) also work. The only halides that do not give Grignards easily are those that are too reactive ($\alpha$-haloacids, $\beta$-haloketones — they decompose).

**⚠️ Where Students Get Stuck**

Two common mistakes. **One:** thinking that C1 and C4 are the same. They are both terminal methyls of a 5-carbon chain, but C1 is attached to the branched C2 (so its parent structure has 2 methyl neighbours via C2) and C4 is attached to the methylene C3 (very different environment). Different = count separately. **Two:** excluding the tertiary RBr because students think $3^\circ$ Grignards "don't form". They do form, just more sluggishly. The question asks for maximum count, so include the tertiary.

$\boxed{\text{Answer: (a) } 4}$

questions.stem:
The ascending order of relative rate of solvolysis of following compounds is:
![image](https://pub-2ff04ffcdd1247b6b8d19c44c1dfe553.r2.dev/questions/6d1dc974-2687-44b5-a968-e7d2e44d19e6/svg/1772176359671_Chem_20260226_140021_592744000_ab0163cb.svg)
questions.explanation:
**🧠 Solvolysis = SN1 = Cation Stability Race. Rank the Cations.**

Read the question carefully. "Solvolysis" means the solvent itself acts as the (weak) nucleophile — water, alcohol, or formic acid attacks the substrate. Weak nucleophile + polar protic solvent = textbook **SN1 conditions**. So the rate depends entirely on **how stable the carbocation intermediate is**.

So the question becomes: rank the four carbocations formed when each compound loses $\ce{Br}$. Most stable cation $\rightarrow$ fastest solvolysis.

**🗺️ Working It Out**

Cation stability ordering, lowest to highest:

- **D (plain $2^\circ$):** weakest. Only $\sigma$-hyperconjugation from 2-3 $\alpha$-H groups.
- **A ($3^\circ$ alkyl):** more stable than D — three alkyl substituents donate via hyperconjugation.
- **B ($2^\circ$ benzylic):** more stable than A — resonance into one aromatic ring spreads positive charge over 4 carbons of the ring.
- **C (dibenzylic $2^\circ$):** most stable — two aromatic rings carry the charge by resonance.

So ascending rate: $\text{D} < \text{A} < \text{B} < \text{C}$. That is option (b).

**⚡ The Smart Move**

For SN1/solvolysis rate problems, the **only** thing that matters is cation stability. Build the ranking ladder by stability factors and apply in order:
1. **Resonance** (benzylic, allylic, dibenzylic, triphenylmethyl) — strongest stabiliser.
2. **Alkyl substitution** ($3^\circ > 2^\circ > 1^\circ$) — hyperconjugation.
3. **Inductive effects** (alpha-O, alpha-N donate; alpha-halo, alpha-CF$_3$ withdraw).

Resonance beats alkyl substitution: a $2^\circ$ benzylic cation is more stable than a $3^\circ$ alkyl cation. That comparison (B vs A here) is the trickiest part of this question.

**⚠️ Where Students Get Stuck**

Two common mistakes. **One:** ranking A above B. Students see "tertiary" and think it must be the most stable. But $2^\circ$ benzylic cations beat $3^\circ$ alkyl cations because resonance > hyperconjugation. **Two:** putting D somewhere in the middle. D is plain $2^\circ$ alkyl — no resonance, no extra hyperconjugation beyond what every $2^\circ$ has. It is the slowest by a clear margin.

$\boxed{\text{Answer: (b) — D < A < B < C}}$

questions.stem:
The structure of the major product formed in the following reaction is:

![image](https://pub-2ff04ffcdd1247b6b8d19c44c1dfe553.r2.dev/questions/d4d7a14c-b9fd-4efe-a782-b77ff5c2e24e/svg/1772176415048_Chem_20260226_140126_719706000_e62d453d.svg)
questions.explanation:
**🧠 AgCN Gives Isocyanide (R-NC). KCN Gives Nitrile (R-CN). Don't Confuse Them.**

Read the substrate carefully. The molecule has **three** halogen-bearing positions: an aryl-$\ce{I}$, an aryl-$\ce{Br}$, and a benzylic $\ce{-CH2-Cl}$. The reagent is $\ce{AgCN}$. Which position reacts, and what product comes out?

Two rules to apply.

**Rule 1 — aryl halides do not undergo SN with AgCN at normal temperatures.** The C-X bond in aryl-Cl, aryl-Br, aryl-I is strong (partial double-bond character from ring resonance) and cannot be cleaved by a soft nucleophile like CN at room temperature. So the aryl-I and aryl-Br positions are spectators here. Only the benzylic $\ce{-CH2-Cl}$ reacts.

**Rule 2 — AgCN is an "N-bonded" cyanide source.** Cyanide is an **ambident nucleophile**: it can attack with either C or N. With ionic alkali metal cyanides ($\ce{KCN}$, $\ce{NaCN}$), the harder C-end attacks $\rightarrow$ product is the **nitrile** $\ce{R-CN}$. With $\ce{AgCN}$, the silver coordinates strongly with the C-end (Ag-C covalent character), leaving the **N-end** free to attack the substrate $\rightarrow$ product is the **isocyanide** $\ce{R-NC}$.

**⚡ The Smart Move**

Lock in this contrast for ambident nucleophiles:
- $\ce{KCN}$ / $\ce{NaCN}$ $\rightarrow$ alkyl **nitrile** $\ce{R-CN}$ (C attacks).
- $\ce{AgCN}$ $\rightarrow$ alkyl **isocyanide** $\ce{R-NC}$ (N attacks).
- $\ce{KNO2}$ $\rightarrow$ alkyl **nitrite** $\ce{R-O-N=O}$ (O attacks).
- $\ce{AgNO2}$ $\rightarrow$ **nitro** compound $\ce{R-NO2}$ (N attacks).

The pattern: alkali metal cation $\rightarrow$ "harder" end of nucleophile (C, O); silver $\rightarrow$ "softer" end of nucleophile (N). HSAB principle in one line.

**⚠️ Where Students Get Stuck**

Two common pitfalls. **One:** picking option (c) (the nitrile $\ce{R-CN}$) because students do not remember the AgCN vs KCN distinction. **Two:** thinking that aryl-Br or aryl-I would react with AgCN. They do — but only under harsh conditions (high T, sometimes Cu catalyst). At normal room-temperature SN conditions, aryl halides are spectators.

$\boxed{\text{Answer: (b) — benzylic } \ce{-CH2-NC} \text{; aryl-I and aryl-Br unchanged}}$

questions.stem:
The products A and B in the following reactions, respectively are
![image](https://pub-2ff04ffcdd1247b6b8d19c44c1dfe553.r2.dev/questions/dbf3e1b6-28cd-4f09-8337-814048ff9efe/svg/1772176518370_Chem_20260226_140345_681737000_630f612d.svg)
questions.explanation:
**🧠 AgX-Reagent = Bonds to Soft End = Nucleophile Attacks from N (or N/N)**

Read the substrate: n-propyl bromide ($\ce{CH3-CH2-CH2-Br}$). Two parallel reactions: one with $\ce{AgNO2}$ (silver nitrite) giving A, another with $\ce{AgCN}$ (silver cyanide) giving B. Both reagents are ambident — they have two possible attacking atoms — and silver flips which atom attacks compared to the alkali metal salt.

The rule is the HSAB pattern. Silver is a soft cation; it forms a covalent bond with the soft end of the nucleophile, leaving the harder end free to attack:

- $\ce{AgNO2}$: $\ce{Ag}$ binds the N (softer); the O-end is blocked. So the **N attacks** $\rightarrow$ product is a **nitro compound** ($\ce{R-NO2}$). Compare with $\ce{KNO2}$, where the ionic $\ce{NO2-}$ attacks via its **harder O-end** $\rightarrow$ **alkyl nitrite** ($\ce{R-O-N=O}$).
- $\ce{AgCN}$: $\ce{Ag}$ binds the C (softer); the N-end is free. So the **N attacks** $\rightarrow$ product is an **isocyanide** ($\ce{R-NC}$). Compare with $\ce{KCN}$: ionic $\ce{CN-}$ attacks via **C** $\rightarrow$ **nitrile** ($\ce{R-CN}$).

**🗺️ Working It Out**


**⚡ The Smart Move**

Lock the four-way pattern into memory:
|     | Alkali metal salt (ionic) | Silver salt (covalent) |
|---|---|---|
| $\ce{NO2-}$ | $\ce{R-O-N=O}$ nitrite (O attacks) | $\ce{R-NO2}$ nitro (N attacks) |
| $\ce{CN-}$ | $\ce{R-CN}$ nitrile (C attacks) | $\ce{R-NC}$ isocyanide (N attacks) |

One-line rule: **Ag pulls the soft end of the nucleophile out of play, so the hard end attacks.** For $\ce{NO2-}$, harder atom = N. For $\ce{CN-}$, harder atom = N. Both happen to be nitrogen in these two cases.

**⚠️ Where Students Get Stuck**

Two pitfalls. **One:** confusing nitrite ($\ce{R-O-N=O}$, an ester of HONO) with nitro ($\ce{R-NO2}$, with N bonded to C). They differ only in the atom attached to the alkyl carbon. **Two:** thinking $\ce{AgCN}$ gives the nitrile because "$\ce{CN}$" is written that way. The "Ag-CN" notation is just convention — what matters is which end of the cyanide is free for nucleophilic attack, and with silver, the N-end is.

$\boxed{\text{Answer: (d) — A = } \ce{CH3CH2CH2NO2}\text{, B = } \ce{CH3CH2CH2NC}}$

questions.stem:
The product B formed in the following reaction sequence is:
(Major product)
![image](https://pub-2ff04ffcdd1247b6b8d19c44c1dfe553.r2.dev/questions/9dd0ed55-e710-421f-a997-b49e7d72a39e/svg/1772176536206_Chem_20260226_140353_668768000_63ce5199.svg)
questions.explanation:
**🧠 Markovnikov + Benzylic Cation = HCl Lands on the Aryl-Side Carbon**

**The HCl step uses Markovnikov.** The proton goes wherever it leaves the **more stable cation** on the other carbon. The two candidate cations are:
- $\ce{Ar-CH^+-CH2-CH3}$ — secondary **benzylic** cation (stabilised by resonance into the aromatic ring).
- $\ce{Ar-CH2-CH^+-CH3}$ — plain secondary cation (no benzylic stabilisation).

Benzylic wins by a big margin. So $\ce{H+}$ adds to the carbon farther from the ring (the one with $\ce{CH3}$), and $\ce{Cl}$ ends up on the benzylic carbon. **A = $\ce{Ar-CHCl-CH2-CH3}$** = 1-(p-tolyl)-1-chloropropane.

**⚡ The Smart Move**

For the AgCN step, remember: **AgCN $\rightarrow$ isocyanide ($\ce{R-NC}$), KCN $\rightarrow$ nitrile ($\ce{R-CN}$)**. If the question used $\ce{KCN}$ here, the answer would be the nitrile (option c). The choice of metal cation flips the product.

**⚠️ Where Students Get Stuck**

Two traps. **One:** picking option (c) (the nitrile) instead of (d). Students who memorise "$\ce{CN}$ goes on alkyl halide" without distinguishing the silver vs alkali metal cases will get this wrong every time. **Two:** putting Cl on the wrong carbon in the first step. Some students apply Markovnikov mechanically without thinking about benzylic stabilisation. The benzylic carbon is the one attached to the ring — that is where Cl should land, regardless of how many alkyl groups are around.

$\boxed{\text{Answer: (d) — } p\text{-tolyl-CH(NC)-CH}_2\text{-CH}_3 \text{ (benzylic isocyanide)}}$

questions.stem:
The major product of the following reaction is:
![image](https://pub-2ff04ffcdd1247b6b8d19c44c1dfe553.r2.dev/questions/f25ce9ec-6871-4a2f-8fca-5e423136d5e9/svg/1772176607830_Chem_20260226_140421_953130000_1be6743f.svg)
questions.explanation:
**🧠 Two Halides + Excess Strong Base + Heat = Double E2 = Conjugated Diene**

The molecule is 3,5-dibromo-2-phenylheptane. Two $\ce{Br}$ atoms on a 7-carbon chain, with a phenyl group on C2. Excess alc. KOH + heat is the most aggressive E2 condition. So **both** halides will be eliminated, giving a **diene**.

The question becomes: which diene? Zaitsev says the more substituted alkene wins; in conjugated systems, the conjugated diene is far more stable than any isolated diene. So we want to find the E2 product where both new $\ce{C=C}$ bonds are **conjugated with each other and with the phenyl ring**.

**⚡ The Smart Move**

For multi-halide eliminations, after the first $\ce{C=C}$ forms, the next elimination should aim for **conjugation with the existing double bond** wherever possible. The conjugation bonus ($\sim 4$ kcal/mol per overlapping $\pi$ pair) easily overrules the regular Zaitsev "most substituted" rule when the two compete.

In this question the substrate is designed so the conjugated product (a) ranks above the more-substituted-but-non-conjugated alternatives (b), (c), (d). Always check if conjugation is reachable before declaring Zaitsev's winner.

**⚠️ Where Students Get Stuck**

Two pitfalls. **One:** ignoring conjugation and applying Zaitsev mechanically. Students who only look at "how many alkyl substituents on each alkene C" may pick option (b) or (d), which have fewer C-C bonds in conjugation with the styrene. **Two:** numbering the chain from the wrong end. Always number so the phenyl substituent (the principal substituent here) gets the lowest locant — that fixes phenyl at C2, not C6, ruling out options (b) and (d).

$\boxed{\text{Answer: (a) — 2-phenylhepta-2,4-diene (cross-conjugated with phenyl)}}$

questions.stem: Which among the following halides will generate the most stable carbocation in the nucleophilic substitution reaction?
questions.explanation:
**🧠 Resonance Beats Hyperconjugation — Three Phenyl Rings > One > Allyl > Plain Alkyl**

Read the question carefully. We are picking the alkyl halide that gives the **most stable carbocation** during nucleophilic substitution (SN1 here, since stable cations are the key). So just rank the cations.

The four candidate cations (formed when $\ce{Br}$ leaves):

- **(a)** Allyl: $\ce{CH2=CH-CH2+}$. Stabilised by resonance to give $\ce{+CH2-CH=CH2}$. Two equivalent resonance structures.
- **(b)** Cyclohex-2-enyl: same as allyl but inside a ring — secondary allyl cation. Roughly similar stability to (a).
- **(c)** Benzyl: $\ce{Ph-CH2+}$. Stabilised by resonance into one aromatic ring (multiple resonance structures across the ring carbons).
- **(d)** Trityl (triphenylmethyl): $\ce{Ph3C+}$. Stabilised by resonance into **three** aromatic rings simultaneously. The most stable carbocation in undergraduate organic chemistry.

**🗺️ Working It Out**

Order of stability (least to most): $\text{allyl (a)} \approx \text{cyclic allyl (b)} < \text{benzyl (c)} < \text{trityl (d)}$.

Trityl wins by a wide margin — the trityl cation is so stable that the corresponding bromide ($\ce{Ph3CBr}$) ionises spontaneously in polar solvents (it is actually used as a stable cation reagent in some reactions). Trityl beats benzyl by $\sim 10$ kcal/mol; benzyl beats allyl by $\sim 5$ kcal/mol.

**⚡ The Smart Move**

For "most stable cation" questions, count the number of independent resonance contributors:
- Allyl: 2 contributors.
- Benzyl: 4 contributors (one starting position + 3 ring positions).
- Trityl: many — 3 rings, each contributing 3 ring positions.

More contributors = more delocalisation = more stable cation = faster ionisation in SN1.

Also note: aryl halides ($\ce{Ph-Br}$ directly bonded to the ring) do NOT ionise — the resulting "phenyl cation" is anti-aromatic and very unstable. The rule above is for halides on $sp^3$ carbons next to aromatic rings (benzyl, trityl), not on the ring itself.

**⚠️ Where Students Get Stuck**

The trap is choosing benzyl (c) because it is "obvious" and stable. Trityl looks more crowded and students avoid it. But the stabilisation is what counts — three phenyl groups spreading positive charge across many carbons beats one phenyl every time. Whenever you see the trityl ($\ce{Ph3C-}$) framework in a halide, that is almost certainly the cation answer.

$\boxed{\text{Answer: (d) — trityl bromide gives the most stable carbocation}}$

questions.stem:
Given below are two statements:

**Statement-I:** The conversion proceeds well in the less polar medium.
$$\text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{CH}_2\text{CH}_2\text{Cl} \xrightarrow{\text{HO}^-} \text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{CH}_2\text{CH}_2\text{OH} + \text{Cl}^-$$

**Statement-II:** The conversion proceeds well in the more polar medium.
$$\text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{CH}_2\text{CH}_2\text{Cl} \xrightarrow{\text{R}_3\overset{..}{\text{N}}} \text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{CH}_2\text{CH}_2\text{N}^+\text{R}_3\text{Cl}^-$$

(a) Both statement I and statement II are true
(b) Both statement I and statement II are false
(c) Statement I is false but statement II is true
(d) Statement I is true but statement II is false

options.text: Both statement I and statement II are true
Both statement I and statement II are false
Statement I is false but statement II is true
Statement I is true but statement II is false

questions.explanation:
**Statement I — SN2 with OH⁻:**

$$\text{R-Cl} + \text{OH}^- \rightarrow \text{R-OH} + \text{Cl}^-$$

Reactants: charged (OH⁻, high charge density)
Transition state: $[\text{HO}\cdots\text{C}\cdots\text{Cl}]^-$ — charge **dispersed** (lower charge density)

A **less polar** solvent better stabilises the dispersed-charge TS relative to the concentrated-charge reactant → **reaction proceeds faster in less polar medium** → ✅ TRUE

**Statement II — Menshutkin reaction (SN2):**

$$\text{R-Cl} + \text{R}_3\text{N} \rightarrow [\text{R}_3\overset{+}{\text{N}}\cdots\text{C}\cdots\text{Cl}^-]^{\ddagger} \rightarrow \text{R}_3\overset{+}{\text{N-R}} + \text{Cl}^-$$

Reactants: **neutral** (low charge density)
Transition state: **charge developing** (higher charge density)
Products: **ionic** (highest charge density)

A **more polar** solvent stabilises the charge-developing TS → lowers activation energy → **reaction proceeds faster in more polar medium** → ✅ TRUE

**Answer: (a) Both statements are true**

questions.stem: Example of vinylic halide is
questions.explanation:
**🧠 Look Where the Halogen Sits — On the $sp^2$ Carbon of $\ce{C=C}$ Means Vinylic**

For "identify the X halide type" questions, do this in 10 seconds: locate the halogen, then look at the carbon it is attached to. If that carbon has a $\ce{C=C}$ on it $\rightarrow$ **vinylic**. If that carbon is one bond away from a $\ce{C=C}$ $\rightarrow$ **allylic**. If the carbon is part of a benzene ring $\rightarrow$ **aryl**. If the carbon is one bond from a benzene ring $\rightarrow$ **benzylic**. Nothing else to check.
![image](https://pub-2ff04ffcdd1247b6b8d19c44c1dfe553.r2.dev/shared/image/1779626976947_Screenshot_2026-05-24_at_6.16.10_PM_0d1586ed.webp)
**⚠️ Where Students Get Stuck**

The common mistake is to call any compound with $\ce{C=C}$ and $\ce{X}$ a "vinylic halide". That is wrong — option (b) here has a $\ce{C=C}$ and an $\ce{X}$ in the same molecule, but the $\ce{X}$ is one carbon away from the double bond. That is allylic, not vinylic. Always check the **hybridisation of the carbon bearing $\ce{X}$**, not just the presence of a nearby $\ce{C=C}$.

$\boxed{\text{Answer: (a) — vinylic halide (}\ce{X}\text{ on } sp^2 \text{ carbon of } \ce{C=C}\text{)}}$

questions.stem:
Identify product A and product B:
![image](https://pub-2ff04ffcdd1247b6b8d19c44c1dfe553.r2.dev/questions/c2c66ae5-50dd-4aad-b804-9e289997b2d6/svg/1772174030151_Chem_20260226_132259_224806000_4ee2f621.svg)
questions.explanation:
**🧠 Light Means Radical Substitution at the Allyl Position. No Light, Polar Solvent Means Ionic Addition.**
![image](https://pub-2ff04ffcdd1247b6b8d19c44c1dfe553.r2.dev/shared/image/1779628089801_Screenshot_2026-05-24_at_6.17.12_PM_55b37ae8.webp)
Read the conditions carefully. The same substrate (cyclohexene) is treated with the same reagent ($\ce{Cl2}$) in two completely different conditions: **hν** vs **$\ce{CCl4}$** (dark). The two conditions force two completely different mechanisms.

- **hν (light):** $\ce{Cl2}$ splits homolytically into two $\ce{Cl}^\bullet$ radicals. These prefer to abstract the **allylic H** (because the resulting allylic radical is resonance-stabilised). So product A is an **allylic chloride with the $\ce{C=C}$ intact**.
- **$\ce{CCl4}$ (no light, non-polar solvent):** $\ce{Cl2}$ stays as a polar molecule. It adds **across the $\ce{C=C}$** via a cyclic chloronium-ion intermediate. Product B is a **vicinal dichloride with no double bond**.



Same reagent, opposite mechanism, opposite product. JEE/NEET examiners love this exact pair of conditions.

**⚠️ Where Students Get Stuck**

The most common mistake is to forget that **light flips the mechanism**. Without light, halogens go ionic (electrophilic addition to the $\pi$ system). With light, they go radical (allylic H abstraction). Students who solve hundreds of "$\ce{Cl2}/\ce{CCl4}$ across $\ce{C=C}$" problems on autopilot sometimes apply addition to the hν case too — which is wrong. Read the condition above the arrow first.

$\boxed{\text{Answer: (d) — A is 3-chlorocyclohex-1-ene (allylic), B is 1,2-dichlorocyclohexane (addition)}}$

questions.stem: The correct statement regarding nucleophilic substitution reaction in a chiral alkyl halide is:

options.text: Retention occurs in $S_N1$ reaction and inversion occurs in $S_N2$ reaction.
Racemisation occurs in $S_N1$ reaction and retention occurs in $S_N2$ reaction.
Racemisation occurs in both $S_N1$ and $S_N2$ reactions.
Racemisation occurs in $S_N1$ reaction and inversion occurs in $S_N2$ reaction.

questions.explanation:
**🧠 SN1 Goes Through a Planar Cation $\rightarrow$ Racemisation. SN2 Is Backside Attack $\rightarrow$ Inversion.**

This is a fundamental stereochemistry question. The answer comes from the **geometry of each mechanism's transition state**.

**SN1:** the substrate ionises first to give a flat $sp^2$ carbocation. The nucleophile then attacks from either face with roughly equal probability. The result: a 1:1 mixture of retention and inversion product — i.e., a **racemic mixture**. (In practice, there is often a small inversion bias because the leaving group sits on one face longer than the other — ion-pair effect — but the dominant outcome is racemisation. "Retention" is never the major outcome.)

**SN2:** the nucleophile attacks the back face of the C-X bond, simultaneously pushing the halide off the front. The carbon transitions through a flat $sp^2$ TS with five partial bonds, then flips through to give the product with the opposite stereochemistry from the starting material. This is **Walden inversion**: every successful SN2 reaction on a chiral centre gives the inverted enantiomer.


**⚡ The Smart Move**

Memorise this stereo-fingerprint table once and never re-derive it:

| Mechanism | Geometry | Stereochemical outcome (chiral substrate) |
|---|---|---|
| SN1 | planar carbocation, attack from either face | racemisation (≈ 1:1) |
| SN2 | backside attack, Walden inversion | clean inversion |
| SNi | retention (cyclic 4-membered TS, e.g. $\ce{SOCl2}$) | retention |

That third row is why $\ce{SOCl2}$ converts alcohols to alkyl chlorides with **retention** of configuration (not racemisation or inversion). It is a separate mechanism (SNi) and a favourite JEE/NEET distraction.

**⚠️ Where Students Get Stuck**

Two common mistakes. **One:** thinking SN1 gives retention because "the LG just leaves, the nucleophile comes back". The leaving group does not "come back" — once the cation is formed, it is flat and either face is open. The nucleophile picks a face at random. So you get a mixture, not retention. **Two:** thinking SN2 gives racemisation because "any substitution randomises stereo". SN2 is the textbook example of a stereospecific reaction — backside attack means clean inversion, no mixture.

$\boxed{\text{Answer: (d) — Racemisation in SN1; inversion in SN2}}$

questions.stem:
Which among the following halide/s will not show $S_N1$ reaction:
![image](https://pub-2ff04ffcdd1247b6b8d19c44c1dfe553.r2.dev/questions/3298d87d-8b81-45ca-b541-0867278f84d4/svg/1772177086873_Chem_20260226_140500_762986000_7a22b6b1.svg)
questions.explanation:
**🧠 Vinyl Halides Don't Do SN1 — the Vinyl Cation Is Too Unstable to Form**

Read the four halides slowly. We have to find the one (or ones) that will NOT ionise to form a carbocation. SN1 needs a stable cation; if the would-be cation is high-energy, the reaction stalls.

Look at each candidate:

- **(A)** allyl chloride, $\ce{CH2=CH-CH2-Cl}$ — the Cl is on an $sp^3$ carbon next to a $\ce{C=C}$. Loss of Cl gives the **allyl cation**, stabilised by resonance. SN1 ✓.
- **(B)** 1-chloropropene, $\ce{CH3-CH=CH-Cl}$ — the Cl is on an $sp^2$ carbon of a $\ce{C=C}$. This is a **vinyl halide**. Loss of Cl would give a **vinyl cation** — an sp-hybridised cation that is anti-aromatic in character and very high energy. SN1 **NOT** observed.
- **(C)** benzyl chloride, $\ce{Ph-CH2-Cl}$ — Cl on $sp^3$ benzylic carbon. Loss of Cl gives the benzyl cation, resonance-stabilised by the ring. SN1 ✓.
- **(D)** isopropyl chloride, $\ce{(CH3)2CH-Cl}$ — Cl on a secondary $sp^3$ carbon. Loss of Cl gives a secondary alkyl cation, OK by hyperconjugation. SN1 ✓ (slow but real).

**🖼️ Reading the Structures**

The key distinction is the hybridisation of the C bearing Cl:
- $sp^3$ + resonance stabilisation $\rightarrow$ great SN1 (A, C).
- $sp^3$ + alkyl substitution $\rightarrow$ workable SN1 (D — secondary).
- $sp^2$ (vinyl) $\rightarrow$ NO SN1 (B).

**🗺️ Working It Out**

Only **B (vinyl chloride)** fails the SN1 test. The vinyl cation $\ce{CH3-CH=CH+}$ is one of the most unstable carbocations — the empty $p$-orbital is in the plane of the $\pi$ system, perpendicular to the $\ce{C=C}$ $\pi$-bond, so resonance into the alkene doesn't help. Vinyl halides are notoriously inert to SN1 (and to SN2, for that matter — they generally only react via addition or coupling).

**⚡ The Smart Move**

Always check the hybridisation of the C bearing the halide before predicting SN1/SN2:
- $sp^3$ C: usual SN rules apply ($3^\circ > 2^\circ$ for SN1; $1^\circ > 2^\circ$ for SN2).
- $sp^2$ C (vinyl, aryl): **neither SN1 nor SN2** occurs at normal conditions. The C-X bond is too strong, the cation is too unstable, and there is no $\sigma^*$ orbital pointing the right way for backside attack.
- $sp$ C (alkynyl-X, rare): similarly inert.

This rule rules out vinyl and aryl halides from SN reactivity instantly — no need to compute resonance.

**⚠️ Where Students Get Stuck**

The classic mistake is to pick **D (isopropyl chloride)** because students think "secondary is in between primary and tertiary, maybe SN1 doesn't work". It does — slowly, but it does. The non-starter is the **vinyl halide**, not the secondary alkyl halide. Whenever you see a $\ce{C=C-X}$ motif (Cl, Br, I on an alkene C), that compound is essentially inert to both SN1 and SN2. Mark it as the exception and move on.

$\boxed{\text{Answer: (b) — vinyl chloride does not show SN1}}$

questions.stem: Alkyl halide is converted into alkyl isocyanide by reaction with
questions.explanation:
**🧠 AgCN Gives Isocyanide. Alkali Metal Cyanide Gives Nitrile. Memorise the Pair.**

This is a direct recall question. Alkyl halides + cyanide reagent can give two different products depending on **which end** of the ambident cyanide nucleophile attacks the carbon:

- **C attacks** $\rightarrow$ **nitrile** ($\ce{R-CN}$).
- **N attacks** $\rightarrow$ **isocyanide** ($\ce{R-NC}$).

The reagent decides which end attacks:

- **$\ce{KCN}$ / $\ce{NaCN}$ / $\ce{NH4CN}$** (ionic, free $\ce{CN-}$): the more nucleophilic **C-end** attacks $\rightarrow$ **nitrile**.
- **$\ce{AgCN}$** (covalent Ag-C bond): the C-end is tied up with silver; the **N-end** is the only nucleophile free for attack $\rightarrow$ **isocyanide**.

**🗺️ Working It Out**

The question asks for the reagent that converts alkyl halide to alkyl **isocyanide** ($\ce{R-NC}$). That is **$\ce{AgCN}$** — option (d).

Options (a) $\ce{NaCN}$ and (c) $\ce{KCN}$ are alkali metal cyanides — both give the nitrile, not the isocyanide. Option (b) $\ce{NH4CN}$ is also an ionic cyanide and gives the nitrile.

**⚡ The Smart Move**

The general HSAB pattern across ambident nucleophiles, with silver vs alkali metals:

| Nucleophile | Alkali metal salt | Silver salt |
|---|---|---|
| $\ce{CN-}$ | $\ce{R-CN}$ (nitrile, C attacks) | $\ce{R-NC}$ (isocyanide, N attacks) |
| $\ce{NO2-}$ | $\ce{R-O-N=O}$ (nitrite, O attacks) | $\ce{R-NO2}$ (nitro, N attacks) |
| $\ce{SCN-}$ | $\ce{R-SCN}$ (thiocyanate, S attacks) | $\ce{R-NCS}$ (isothiocyanate, N attacks) |

In every case, silver coordinates with the **soft end** (C, N, S) and the **N-end** ends up doing the attack. Three rules, one underlying pattern.

**⚠️ Where Students Get Stuck**

Students who do not remember which way the silver flips the product end up picking $\ce{KCN}$ (option c) by default. The mnemonic "**A**gCN $\rightarrow$ Iso(**A**)cyanide" works for some — silver gives the iso- form, alkali metal gives the regular form.

Also note: this rule is about the SN substitution product. If the reaction goes through SN1 (e.g., tertiary or benzylic halides), even $\ce{KCN}$ can give some isocyanide because the carbocation grabs whichever end of cyanide is statistically nearby. But at standard JEE/NEET conditions (primary/secondary alkyl halide + AgCN), you get the isocyanide cleanly.

$\boxed{\text{Answer: (d) AgCN}}$

questions.stem:
The product (C) in the below mentioned reaction is:
![image](https://pub-2ff04ffcdd1247b6b8d19c44c1dfe553.r2.dev/questions/090a8c8f-afec-4d86-8023-a0700d6143d6/svg/1772177523968_Chem_20260226_140612_493040000_c40b3ab8.svg)
questions.explanation:
**🧠 Track Each Step — Elim, Markovnikov Add, Subst. The Cl Moves Carbon-to-Carbon Along the Way.**

Read the reaction sequence carefully. Three reagents in a row act on n-propyl bromide: KOH(alc) with heat gives A (E2 elimination); HBr gives B (Markovnikov addition); KOH(aq) with heat gives C (SN substitution).

Track the structure after each transformation. The subtle point: alc. KOH does **elimination**, aq. KOH does **substitution**, and HBr in between moves the bromine to a different carbon along the way.

**🗺️ Working It Out**

**First, alc. KOH on $\ce{CH3-CH2-CH2-Br}$:** E2 elimination. Only one $\beta$-H position is available (on the central $\ce{CH2}$). Product **A = propene** ($\ce{CH3-CH=CH2}$).

**Next, HBr on propene:** Markovnikov addition. The proton adds to the less substituted carbon ($\ce{=CH2}$) and Br to the more substituted carbon (the central one), via the more stable secondary carbocation. Product **B = 2-bromopropane** ($\ce{CH3-CHBr-CH3}$).

**Finally, aq. KOH on 2-bromopropane:** SN substitution. Aqueous KOH supplies $\ce{OH-}$ as the nucleophile; the secondary alkyl halide undergoes substitution. The $\ce{Br}$ on C2 is replaced by $\ce{OH}$. Product **C = propan-2-ol** ($\ce{CH3-CH(OH)-CH3}$, isopropanol).

That matches option (d).

The other options test what students might pick if they confuse one step:
- (a) propan-1-ol: would only form if HBr was anti-Markovnikov (peroxide) AND aq. KOH was substitution. Wrong because the peroxide condition is not there.
- (b) propene: confusing A with C — propene is the alkene intermediate, not the final alcohol.
- (c) propyne: would require two consecutive eliminations and an alkene-to-alkyne step, not in the sequence.

**⚡ The Smart Move**

Three reagent recognition rules to lock in:
1. **Alc. KOH** $\rightarrow$ E2 elimination (gives alkene).
2. **Aq. KOH** $\rightarrow$ SN substitution (gives alcohol).
3. **HBr** alone (no peroxide) $\rightarrow$ Markovnikov addition (Br on more substituted C).

Notice how HBr in the middle **shifts the Br position** from C1 of propyl bromide (the start) to C2 (a more substituted carbon) of the final isopropanol. That is the "trick" the question is testing — students who treat the propyl group as unchanged will get propan-1-ol (option a).

**⚠️ Where Students Get Stuck**

The most common mistake is using **aq. KOH for elimination and alc. KOH for substitution** — the two are mixed up. Memorise: "alc. = alkene, aq. = alcohol" (rhyming hook). Second pitfall: forgetting that HBr addition to propene puts Br on the central carbon (Markovnikov), not on the terminal carbon. Final result: the OH is on the central carbon of propan-2-ol, not the terminal carbon of propan-1-ol.

$\boxed{\text{Answer: (d) — Propan-2-ol}}$

questions.stem:
![image](https://pub-2ff04ffcdd1247b6b8d19c44c1dfe553.r2.dev/questions/a6e2f372-a48a-4529-8b6b-da6ec6b374b2/svg/1772177766856_Chem_20260226_140650_946979000_f1862aed.svg)
Acid D formed in above reaction is:
questions.explanation:
**🧠 Track Each Step — A 2-Carbon Substrate Grows to 4 Carbons via Double CN Addition**

Read the reagent sequence carefully. Ethyl bromide ($\ce{C2H5Br}$, 2 carbons) becomes acid D after four reagents: alc. KOH (E2 elimination); $\ce{Br2}/\ce{CCl4}$ (electrophilic addition); KCN excess (double SN2 substitution); $\ce{H3O+}$ excess (nitrile hydrolysis).

Each step does ONE specific transformation. Track the structure carefully.

**🗺️ Working It Out**

Starting from $\ce{CH3-CH2-Br}$:

**alc. KOH (E2):** removes a $\beta$-H from $\ce{CH3}$ + the Br from the adjacent C. Product **A = ethene** ($\ce{CH2=CH2}$). Two carbons.

**$\ce{Br2}/\ce{CCl4}$ (addition):** electrophilic addition across the $\ce{C=C}$ via bromonium ion (anti-addition). Product **B = 1,2-dibromoethane** ($\ce{BrCH2-CH2Br}$). Still two carbons, now with TWO Br.

**KCN (excess, SN2 twice):** each Br is displaced by cyanide via SN2. The C-end of $\ce{CN-}$ attacks (because KCN is ionic). With excess KCN, both Br's are replaced. Product **C = succinonitrile** ($\ce{NC-CH2-CH2-CN}$). Now FOUR carbons total (2 original + 2 from CN).

**$\ce{H3O+}$ (excess, nitrile hydrolysis):** each $\ce{-CN}$ is hydrolysed to $\ce{-COOH}$ via amide intermediate. The carbon of CN stays attached; only the N is replaced by =O and -OH. Product **D = succinic acid** ($\ce{HOOC-CH2-CH2-COOH}$), the 4-carbon dicarboxylic acid.

That matches option (b).

**⚡ The Smart Move**

Track the **carbon count** at every step. Reactions that ADD carbons to the chain are rare and noticeable — in this sequence, only KCN adds carbons (CN brings one C per Br it replaces). Eliminations, additions, and hydrolyses don't add C count.

Starting from $\ce{C2H5Br}$ (2 carbons) and adding 2 carbons via 2 KCN substitutions gives a 4-carbon acid. The four 4-C diacid candidates in JEE:
- **Oxalic acid** $\ce{HOOC-COOH}$ (2 C, dicarboxyl on adjacent C — no $\ce{CH2}$ spacer).
- **Malonic acid** $\ce{HOOC-CH2-COOH}$ (3 C, one $\ce{CH2}$ spacer).
- **Succinic acid** $\ce{HOOC-CH2-CH2-COOH}$ (4 C, two $\ce{CH2}$ spacers). Our answer.
- **Glutaric acid** $\ce{HOOC-(CH2)3-COOH}$ (5 C, three $\ce{CH2}$ spacers).

The number of $\ce{CH2}$ between the two $\ce{COOH}$ groups tells you the diacid by name.

**⚠️ Where Students Get Stuck**

Two pitfalls. **One:** picking malonic acid (3 C) by accident, ignoring that there are TWO Br's in B and each gets a CN. The pathway naturally produces succinic acid (4-C diacid). **Two:** stopping at the nitrile (C = succinonitrile) and calling it the acid. The $\ce{H3O+}$ excess step is what converts the nitriles to acids; without it the question would end at C.

$\boxed{\text{Answer: (b) — Succinic acid (HOOC-CH}_2\text{-CH}_2\text{-COOH)}}$

questions.stem:
![image](https://pub-2ff04ffcdd1247b6b8d19c44c1dfe553.r2.dev/questions/9afb0950-4df7-490f-942b-64d0f04f28af/svg/1772177835432_Chem_20260226_140700_448587000_2bb2519f.svg)
Identify B and C and how are A and C related?
![image](https://pub-2ff04ffcdd1247b6b8d19c44c1dfe553.r2.dev/questions/9afb0950-4df7-490f-942b-64d0f04f28af/svg/1772177875106_Chem_20260226_140828_930048000_cb5dd064.svg)
questions.explanation:
**🧠 Aryl Halides Are Spectators — Only the Alkyl Halide Reacts**

Read the substrate carefully. The molecule has **two** $\ce{Br}$ atoms: one on the **aromatic ring** (para position) and one at the **end of the ethyl chain**. These two C-Br bonds behave very differently.

The **aryl $\ce{C-Br}$** is unreactive to alcoholic NaOH at normal temperatures. The aryl-halide bond has partial double-bond character from ring resonance, and there is no $\beta$-H to eliminate on the ring. (Industrial Dow process requires $\sim 300^\circ\text{C}$ and concentrated NaOH — far above what alcoholic NaOH at moderate temperatures provides.)

The **alkyl $\ce{C-Br}$** is reactive. With alcoholic NaOH, E2 elimination removes a $\beta$-H (from the benzylic $\ce{-CH2-}$) and the Br, giving a $\ce{C=C}$.

So B is 4-bromostyrene, with the ring Br intact and the alkyl chain converted to a vinyl group.

**🖼️ Reading the Option Structures**

Each option proposes a (B, C) pair plus the A-vs-C relationship. We need the pair where the ring Br stays put on both B and C, B has a vinyl group, and C has the right Markovnikov regio.

- Options (a) and (b) show ring-OH on both B and C — but aryl-Br does not get converted to aryl-OH under alc. NaOH. Both wrong on B.
- Option (c) shows B = 4-bromostyrene, C = 1-bromo-1-(4-bromophenyl)ethane, and labels A vs C as **position isomers**. Matches the chemistry.
- Option (d) shows B = 4-bromostyrene (correct), C = same as starting A — wrong because HBr/Markovnikov gives a benzylic Br, not a terminal one.

**🗺️ Working It Out**

So **B = 4-bromostyrene** ($\ce{p-Br-C6H4-CH=CH2}$). Then HBr/ether on this styrene-type alkene is Markovnikov addition. The proton adds to the less substituted alkene carbon ($\ce{=CH2}$), giving a more stable benzylic cation; $\ce{Br-}$ then captures the cation. Result: **C = $\ce{p-Br-C6H4-CHBr-CH3}$** (1-bromo-1-(4-bromophenyl)ethane).

Comparing A vs C: A has Br on terminal $sp^3$ carbon (primary); C has Br on benzylic $sp^3$ carbon (secondary). Both have molecular formula $\ce{C8H8Br2}$ and the same carbon skeleton; they differ only in WHERE the second Br sits on the ethyl chain. That makes them **position isomers**.

That matches option (c).

**⚡ The Smart Move**

Three habits for multi-halide problems:
1. Spot the aryl-Br as a spectator under non-extreme conditions. It stays put.
2. Identify the reactive site — only the alkyl halide will undergo SN/E reactions at JEE/NEET conditions.
3. For HBr addition to styrene-type alkenes, the Br always goes to the benzylic position (more stable cation). Memorise this regiochemistry.

The "A↔C" relationship in this question is **position isomers** because both have $\ce{C8H8Br2}$ but the second Br is on a different position of the 2-carbon chain. Don't confuse with "chain isomers" (different carbon skeleton) or "functional group isomers" (different functional groups).

**⚠️ Where Students Get Stuck**

Two traps. **One:** thinking aryl-Br converts to aryl-OH under alc. NaOH. That requires the Dow process conditions, not standard alc. NaOH. Marking options (a) or (b) ignores this. **Two:** putting Br on the wrong carbon when HBr adds to the styrene. Markovnikov + benzylic cation stability puts Br at the benzylic position, not at the terminal $\ce{=CH2}$.

$\boxed{\text{Answer: (c) — B = 4-bromostyrene, C = 1-bromo-1-(4-bromophenyl)ethane, position isomers}}$

questions.stem:
Identify the correct set of reagents or reaction conditions 'X' and 'Y' in the following set of transformation.
![image](https://pub-2ff04ffcdd1247b6b8d19c44c1dfe553.r2.dev/questions/956c4e33-e60b-4f3f-8a05-870e86446ff3/svg/1772178112344_Chem_20260227_131130_412340000_b47ecce2.svg)

options.text: X = dil.aq. NaOH, 20°C, Y = HBr/acetic acid
X = conc.alc. NaOH, 80°C, Y = HBr/acetic acid

questions.explanation:
**🧠 The Bromine Moves From C1 to C2 — That Means Elimination + Markovnikov Addition.**

Read the sequence: $\ce{CH3-CH2-CH2-Br}$ (n-propyl bromide, primary) gets transformed by reagents X then Y to give $\ce{CH3-CHBr-CH3}$ (isopropyl bromide, secondary). The bromine has **moved** from the terminal C1 to the central C2.

The only standard pathway that moves a halogen this way: **E2 elimination first** (to give an alkene), then **Markovnikov HBr addition** (puts Br on the more substituted carbon of the alkene). That fixes the reagents:

- **X** must do E2: alcoholic strong base, heat. Options listed: conc. alc. NaOH at $80^\circ\text{C}$ (works) or dil. aq. NaOH at $20^\circ\text{C}$ (does substitution, not elimination — wrong).
- **Y** must do Markovnikov HBr addition: HBr in a polar protic medium (acetic acid is typical for keeping HBr ionised). Options: HBr/acetic acid (works) or $\ce{Br2}/\ce{CHCl3}$ (adds 2 Br atoms across the $\ce{C=C}$, not 1 — wrong).

**🗺️ Working It Out**

Check each option:
- (a) X = conc. alc. NaOH (good), Y = $\ce{Br2}/\ce{CHCl3}$ (bad). Y would give 1,2-dibromopropane, not 2-bromopropane.
- (b) X = dil. aq. NaOH (bad) — gives propan-1-ol (SN), not propene. Whole pathway wrong.
- (c) X = conc. alc. NaOH (good), Y = HBr/acetic acid (good). **Both correct.**
- (d) Both X and Y wrong.

The intermediate (the product after X) is **propene** ($\ce{CH3-CH=CH2}$), and Markovnikov HBr addition puts Br on the central carbon (the more stable secondary carbocation), giving 2-bromopropane.

**⚡ The Smart Move**

Recognise this "Br moves" pattern instantly. It is a classic synthesis trick: when you need to relocate a halide from one carbon to another, the standard route is alkyl halide $\rightarrow$ alkene (via E2) $\rightarrow$ different alkyl halide (via Markovnikov HX). This shifts the halogen one carbon over, toward the more substituted position.

For the reverse direction (move halide AWAY from the more substituted position), use HBr with peroxide on the alkene to get anti-Markovnikov regiochemistry.

**⚠️ Where Students Get Stuck**

Two pitfalls. **One:** confusing $\ce{Br2}/\ce{CHCl3}$ with $\ce{HBr}$. $\ce{Br2}$ adds across $\ce{C=C}$ to give a vicinal dibromide ($\ce{CHBrCHBr}$), not a single Br substitution. The product wanted is 2-bromopropane (one Br on C2), not 1,2-dibromopropane. **Two:** picking dil. aq. NaOH as X. Aqueous NaOH does SN substitution, giving an alcohol — not the alkene we need for the next step.

$\boxed{\text{Answer: (c) — X = conc. alc. NaOH at 80°C; Y = HBr in acetic acid}}$

questions.stem:
Find out the major product formed from the following reaction.
![image](https://pub-2ff04ffcdd1247b6b8d19c44c1dfe553.r2.dev/questions/956c4e33-e60b-4f3f-8a05-870e86446ff3/svg/1772177970557_Chem_20260226_141433_802811000_b66e9b05.svg)
questions.explanation:
**🧠 Two Br at Allylic Positions + Two Equivalents of Amine = Double SN2 at the Same Allyl Sites**

Read the substrate: a cyclopent-1-ene ring with **two Br** at the allylic positions (C3 and C5). The reagent is dimethylamine $\ce{Me2NH}$, 2 equivalents. With one equivalent of amine per Br, each Br gets substituted cleanly.

The question reduces to: where do the two $\ce{NMe2}$ groups end up?

Dimethylamine is a moderate nucleophile (not super-strong, not super-weak). At allylic positions, two pathways compete:
- **SN2** at each allylic C: clean backside attack, Br leaves, $\ce{Me2N}$ stays at the same carbon. The $\ce{C=C}$ position does not move.
- **SN1** through allyl cation: the cation is resonance-delocalised, the nucleophile could attack at either end of the conjugated allyl system, potentially relocating the $\ce{C=C}$.

For a symmetric 3,5-dibromocyclopent-1-ene, SN2 (clean) is the dominant pathway with a moderate nucleophile, and the product retains the allylic substitution pattern.

**🗺️ Working It Out**

Substitute each Br with $\ce{NMe2}$ via SN2:
- C3 Br $\rightarrow$ C3 $\ce{NMe2}$.
- C5 Br $\rightarrow$ C5 $\ce{NMe2}$.

The $\ce{C=C}$ between C1 and C2 stays put. Final product: **3,5-bis(dimethylamino)cyclopent-1-ene** — option (b).

**🖼️ Reading the Option Structures**

The four options differ in where the $\ce{NMe2}$ groups end up and whether the $\ce{C=C}$ has moved:

- (a) shows one $\ce{NMe2}$ on $sp^3$ C3 and one $\ce{NMe2}$ on $sp^2$ C2 (vinyl amine / "enamine"-type). This is the SN1 product if the allyl cation lets one $\ce{NMe2}$ attack at the migrated end. Possible side product but not the major.
- (b) shows both $\ce{NMe2}$ at the original allylic positions (C3 and C5) with the $\ce{C=C}$ unmoved. Clean SN2 product. ✓
- (c) shows both $\ce{NMe2}$ on adjacent ring carbons (not the original allylic positions) — would require multiple rearrangements; not the major.
- (d) shows similar weirdness; not the SN2 product.

**⚡ The Smart Move**

For "allylic halide + nucleophile" problems, the question is whether the C=C stays put (SN2) or migrates (SN1 via allyl cation). The rule of thumb:
- Moderate nucleophile + good SN2 substrate (small ring, no steric block): clean SN2, C=C unchanged.
- Weak nucleophile + polar protic solvent: SN1 favoured, allyl cation can rearrange.

Here $\ce{Me2NH}$ is moderate, and the cyclopentene allylic positions are not sterically blocked, so SN2 wins cleanly. With 2 equivalents, both Br are replaced at their original positions.

**⚠️ Where Students Get Stuck**

The trap is picking option (a) (the "enamine" product). Students who see allylic Br instinctively assume SN1 and allyl-cation migration. But with a clean nucleophile like dimethylamine and no strong polar protic solvent given, SN2 at the original position is the major pathway. SN1 migration would also give a mixture (R/R, R/S, S/S of the original positions plus some migrated), not a clean single product.

$\boxed{\text{Answer: (b) — 3,5-bis(dimethylamino)cyclopent-1-ene}}$

questions.stem:
![image](https://pub-2ff04ffcdd1247b6b8d19c44c1dfe553.r2.dev/questions/4b7c1ebc-b90e-44fa-a9c7-78fc89287eac/svg/1772178382103_Chem_20260226_141509_497104000_84b2400a.svg)
Major product P is:
questions.explanation:
**🧠 E2 Has Only One Direction Here — The Quaternary Carbon Has No $\beta$-H**

Read the substrate carefully. The molecule is $\ce{Ph-CH2-CHBr-C(CH3)3}$ — 2-bromo-3,3-dimethyl-1-phenylbutane. The carbon bearing Br is C2; the two neighbouring carbons (C1 = $\ce{CH2}$ next to Ph, C3 = the quaternary $\ce{-C(CH3)3}$) are the candidates for $\beta$-H abstraction.

Quick check: C3 is **quaternary** ($\ce{C(CH3)3}$ has 3 methyl groups + 1 chain bond = 4 bonds, **zero H**). E2 cannot remove a $\beta$-H from a quaternary carbon. So the only possible elimination direction is toward C1 (the benzylic $\ce{CH2}$, which has 2 $\beta$-H's). One direction, one product.

**🗺️ Working It Out**

E2 removes a $\beta$-H from the benzylic $\ce{CH2}$ and the Br from C2 simultaneously, giving a $\ce{C=C}$ between C1 and C2. The product is $\ce{Ph-CH=CH-C(CH3)3}$ — **(E)-1-phenyl-3,3-dimethylbut-1-ene** (trans-3,3-dimethyl-1-phenylpropene, the trans-styrene-type alkene with a t-butyl group on the other end).

For the stereochemistry: E2 is anti-periplanar. With a free-rotating open-chain substrate, the more stable trans (E)-alkene dominates because the bulky $\ce{-C(CH3)3}$ and Ph prefer to sit trans to each other in the transition state. So the major product has the styryl-type E configuration with extra conjugation into the ring.

That matches option (b) — the structure showing Ph-$\ce{C=C}$-t-Bu with trans geometry.

**🖼️ Reading the Option Structures**

- (a) shows the wrong constitution — looks like an exocyclic double bond on a benzylic position with a t-Bu on the other side, not the styryl-type alkene we expect.
- (b) shows trans-Ph-$\ce{CH=CH-C(CH3)3}$ — conjugated with the ring, t-Bu on the far end. Matches.
- (c) and (d) show alkenes at the wrong position or with no conjugation. Both are non-Zaitsev or impossible E2 products.

**⚡ The Smart Move**

Three habits for E2 problems with bulky substrates:
1. List the $\beta$-carbons of the C-X centre and check whether each has at least one H.
2. If any $\beta$-C is quaternary, exclude that direction entirely (impossible E2 path).
3. If both $\beta$-directions are open, choose by Zaitsev (more substituted) or in some cases Hofmann (very bulky base).

Here, the quaternary t-butyl carbon blocks one direction completely, so the question is decided by elimination toward the benzylic side. The conjugation with the aromatic ring also makes the styrene-type product especially stable.

**⚠️ Where Students Get Stuck**

Two pitfalls. **One:** trying to eliminate toward the t-Bu side and inventing a product where the C=C is between C2 and C3. That is impossible because C3 has no H. The arrow can't pull off a hydrogen that does not exist. **Two:** ignoring stereochemistry and picking the cis-isomer. The trans-styrene is the thermodynamically and kinetically favoured product because the bulky Ph and t-Bu groups want to sit trans.

$\boxed{\text{Answer: (b) — (E)-1-phenyl-3,3-dimethylbut-1-ene}}$

questions.stem:
Identify the major product in the following reaction.
![image](https://pub-2ff04ffcdd1247b6b8d19c44c1dfe553.r2.dev/questions/f7f5807d-d370-41a0-b50d-3e3a837e1979/svg/1772178786271_Chem_20260226_141542_662639000_1697f58b.svg)
questions.explanation:
**🧠 Tertiary Halide + Alc. NaOH/KOH = E2 Zaitsev = Most Substituted Alkene**

Read the substrate carefully. We have 1-bromo-1-methylcyclopentane (a tertiary alkyl halide on a cyclopentane ring). The reagent is $\ce{OH-}$ dissolved in ethanol — that is the textbook "alcoholic KOH/NaOH" condition for E2 elimination.

Tertiary halides almost always go E2 with alc. base (SN2 is blocked by steric crowding; SN1 with a weak nucleophile like ethanol is slow). The question is just: which alkene is the major (Zaitsev) product?

**🗺️ Working It Out**

The carbon bearing Br has three types of $\beta$-hydrogens:
- Three H's on the $\ce{-CH3}$ substituent.
- Two H's on each of the two adjacent ring carbons (4 ring $\beta$-H total).

Elimination toward the methyl gives **methylenecyclopentane** ($\ce{=CH2}$ exocyclic to the ring) — a disubstituted alkene (=CH$_2$ has only 1 substituent on the C bearing R, the C without R has only H's).

Elimination toward a ring $\ce{-CH2-}$ gives **1-methylcyclopent-1-ene** — a trisubstituted alkene (the C carrying the methyl is sp$^2$, with 2 ring C's and 1 methyl substituent + double bond; that side has 3 substituents).

Zaitsev's rule: the more substituted alkene wins. Trisubstituted (1-methylcyclopent-1-ene) beats disubstituted (methylenecyclopentane) by $\sim$2-3 kcal/mol in alkene stability. So the major product is **1-methylcyclopent-1-ene** — option (c).

**🖼️ Reading the Option Structures**

- (a) methylenecyclopentane (exocyclic =$\ce{CH2}$): Hofmann product, not major under alc. base.
- (b) 1-bromocyclopent-1-ene: vinyl bromide — would mean Br stayed and an H left. Wrong (E2 must lose Br, not retain it).
- (c) 1-methylcyclopent-1-ene (CH$_3$ on sp$^2$ ring C, internal $\ce{C=C}$): Zaitsev major product. ✓
- (d) Methylcyclopentane (no $\ce{C=C}$, no $\ce{OH}$): does not match any product of elimination or substitution.

**⚡ The Smart Move**

For tertiary alkyl halides + alc. KOH/NaOH:
- **Default:** E2 to give the Zaitsev (more substituted) alkene.
- **Hofmann exception:** if the base is bulky (e.g., potassium tert-butoxide), the less substituted alkene wins.

In this question, $\ce{OH-}$ is a small base, so Zaitsev applies. The more substituted alkene (1-methylcyclopent-1-ene) wins.

Also note that for cyclic substrates, $\beta$-H abstraction from the methyl gives an exocyclic alkene, while $\beta$-H abstraction from a ring $\ce{-CH2-}$ gives an endocyclic alkene. Endocyclic alkenes are usually more stable for 5- and 6-rings (no ring strain issues).

**⚠️ Where Students Get Stuck**

Two pitfalls. **One:** picking option (a) (methylenecyclopentane) — the Hofmann/anti-Zaitsev product. That would be the major product only with a bulky base; with $\ce{OH-}$, Zaitsev wins. **Two:** picking option (d) (just methylcyclopentane). That implies losing the Br with no substituent gained, which is not a real reaction outcome.

$\boxed{\text{Answer: (c) — 1-methylcyclopent-1-ene (Zaitsev product)}}$

questions.stem:
![image](https://pub-2ff04ffcdd1247b6b8d19c44c1dfe553.r2.dev/questions/98e981c9-cd39-4cde-ae8b-9f2f474655eb/svg/1772178989952_Chem_20260226_141713_358556000_d04e519b.svg)
Consider the above chemical reaction. Product "A" is:
questions.explanation:
**🧠 Secondary Halide in Aq. NaOH = SN1 With Possible Cation Rearrangement**

Read the substrate carefully. The molecule is cyclohexyl-$\ce{CHCl-CH2-CH3}$ — a secondary alkyl chloride where the C-Cl carbon is attached directly to a cyclohexyl ring on one side and an ethyl group on the other.

Reagent: $\ce{NaOH}$ in $\ce{H2O}$ (aqueous NaOH). For a secondary halide in aqueous conditions, both SN1 and SN2 can occur, but the secondary cation here is positioned NEXT TO a tertiary CH (the cyclohexyl ring carbon bonded to the chain). That sets up a **1,2-hydride shift** to give a more stable tertiary cation. If a rearrangement is possible AND gives a tertiary cation, SN1 with rearrangement usually wins.

**🗺️ Working It Out**

The unrearranged SN1 cation: $\ce{(cyclohexyl)-CH^+-CH2-CH3}$ — secondary, on the chain.

1,2-hydride shift: the cyclohexyl ring carbon has one H. That H migrates with its bonding pair from the ring C to the chain cation, neutralising the chain cation but creating a new cation on the ring C. The new cation is on the ring carbon, which now bears: 2 ring $\ce{CH2}$ neighbours + 1 chain $\ce{CH2CH2CH3}$ + (+) — i.e., **tertiary** (3 alkyl substituents). The hydride shift is highly favourable: a secondary cation rearranges to a tertiary cation, gaining $\sim$10-15 kcal/mol of stability.

Water then attacks the tertiary ring cation, installing $\ce{OH}$ on the ring C. Final product: a cyclohexane ring with $\ce{OH}$ on the ring carbon and the n-propyl chain hanging off the same ring carbon. That is **1-propyl-cyclohexan-1-ol** — option (b).

**🖼️ Reading the Option Structures**

- (a) shows cyclohexyl-$\ce{CH(OH)-CH2-CH3}$ — the unrearranged SN1 product (OH at the original Cl position). This would be major if no rearrangement happened.
- (b) shows 1-propyl-cyclohexan-1-ol — the rearranged tertiary alcohol. Matches our derivation. ✓
- (c) and (d) show alternative isomers that don't fit either SN1 pathway cleanly.

**⚡ The Smart Move**

Whenever you see a secondary or tertiary alkyl halide where the **adjacent carbon has at least one H AND a more substituted environment**, watch for carbocation rearrangement. Two rules:
1. **1,2-hydride shift:** $2^\circ \rightarrow 3^\circ$ or $1^\circ \rightarrow 2^\circ$ rearrangement, when the adjacent C has a $\ce{C-H}$ that can migrate.
2. **1,2-methyl shift:** when there is no migratable H but an alkyl group on the adjacent carbon can move (less common but happens in neopentyl-type systems).

The rule: cations always rearrange toward more stable structures, and this is essentially never "skipped" if a stable rearrangement is available.

**⚠️ Where Students Get Stuck**

The most common mistake is picking option (a) — the unrearranged product — because students don't think about whether a hydride shift can happen. Whenever the original cation is secondary and the adjacent C has an H that would make the cation tertiary, the hydride shift WILL happen. The unrearranged product is essentially never the major outcome under SN1 conditions.

$\boxed{\text{Answer: (b) — 1-propyl-cyclohexan-1-ol (rearranged via 1,2-hydride shift)}}$

questions.stem: Which among the following compounds will undergo fastest $S_N2$ reaction.
questions.explanation:
**🧠 SN2 Rate Order: $1^\circ > 2^\circ > 3^\circ$ — and All Primary Halides Beat Any Secondary**

Read the four halides slowly. SN2 requires backside attack on the C bearing X. The α-carbon must be uncrowded (1° best, 2° workable, 3° essentially blocked). Identify which is primary, secondary, or tertiary.

- **(a)** $\ce{(cyclobutyl)C(CH3)2-Br}$ — a tertiary carbon (3 alkyl + 1 Br, no H). **No SN2.**
- **(b)** 1-bromo-1-methylcyclobutane — a tertiary carbon on the cyclobutane ring (2 ring + 1 methyl + 1 Br). **No SN2.**
- **(c)** $\ce{cyclobutyl-CH2-CH2-Br}$ — a primary carbon (the $\ce{-CH2-Br}$ has 2 H + 1 Br + 1 chain carbon). **SN2 viable, fast.**
- **(d)** $\ce{cyclobutyl-CHBr-CH3}$ — a secondary carbon (1 H + 1 Br + 2 different alkyl substituents). **SN2 possible but slower.**

The fastest SN2 substrate is the primary halide, option (c).

**🗺️ Working It Out**

Compare (c) and (d) directly. Both are open-chain alkyl halides with a cyclobutyl group nearby:
- (c) has cyclobutyl on the $\beta$-carbon (one carbon away from the C-Br). The $\alpha$-C has 2 H and is accessible for backside attack. The $\beta$-cyclobutyl group introduces some steric bulk but is not as bulky as a t-butyl group — SN2 is slowed somewhat but still fast.
- (d) has cyclobutyl directly on the $\alpha$-carbon (same C as the Br). The $\alpha$-C is more crowded (cyclobutyl + methyl + Br + H), which slows SN2 significantly.

For SN2, $\alpha$-bulk matters more than $\beta$-bulk because it directly blocks the backside attack. So (d) is slower than (c) despite both having cyclobutyl somewhere nearby.

The "primary > secondary" rule wins: (c) is faster.

**⚡ The Smart Move**

For "fastest SN2" comparison questions, use a two-pass filter:
1. **Eliminate tertiary halides first** — they don't do SN2.
2. **Among the remaining (primary and secondary)**, count $\alpha$-substituents: fewer = faster. A primary halide with $\beta$-branching (like neopentyl) is the only "primary that doesn't do SN2 well" case — but normal primaries always beat secondaries.

This question has 2 tertiary substrates (a, b) that are eliminated, then a primary (c) vs secondary (d) comparison. Primary wins.

**⚠️ Where Students Get Stuck**

Two pitfalls. **One:** picking option (a) or (b) because they "look like" they could react (visible Br, lots of alkyl groups). Tertiary halides are essentially unreactive in SN2 — students who don't classify the substrate type first end up here. **Two:** picking (d) over (c) because of the "α-position seems normal" reflex. Always categorise by primary/secondary/tertiary first; the rate order is essentially set by that.

$\boxed{\text{Answer: (c) — primary halide (cyclobutyl-CH}_2\text{-CH}_2\text{-Br)}}$

questions.stem:
In the following sequence of reaction, the major products B and C respectively are:
![image](https://pub-2ff04ffcdd1247b6b8d19c44c1dfe553.r2.dev/questions/b3e9d241-35a6-4d2c-9929-9f809e8b66e4/svg/1772179459971_Chem_20260226_141848_790130000_335e9196.svg)
questions.explanation:
**🧠 Track Each Reagent — Wurtz Couples, Grignard Plus D2O Deuterates, CoF2 Swarts-Fluorinates**

Read the reaction sequence carefully. The substrate is a dihalide ($\ce{Cl-cyclobutyl-Br}$, a cyclobutane bearing both Cl and Br at different positions). Three independent reagent combinations are applied:
- $\ce{Na}/\ce{Et2O}$ $\rightarrow$ A: Wurtz-type coupling.
- A + $\ce{Mg}/\ce{Et2O}$, then $\ce{D2O}$ $\rightarrow$ B: Grignard formation + deuteration.
- A + $\ce{CoF2}$ $\rightarrow$ C: Swarts-type halide exchange to fluoride.

We need to identify B and C.

**🗺️ Working It Out**

**Forming A (Wurtz with Na):** $\ce{Na}$ in dry ether reacts with alkyl halides. Among Cl and Br on the same molecule, $\ce{Br}$ is the better leaving group, so Na removes Br first via single-electron transfer. Two molecules of $\ce{Cl-cyclobutyl-Br}$ couple at their Br positions, giving **A = $\ce{Cl-cyclobutyl-cyclobutyl-Cl}$** (bicyclobutyl with Cl on each ring at the original Cl positions). The Br on each molecule is consumed; the Cl on each remains.

**Forming B (Grignard + $\ce{D2O}$):** A has two C-Cl bonds (one per ring). Mg inserts into each C-Cl bond, giving a bis-Grignard. Then $\ce{D2O}$ quenches both Grignard carbons with D (each C-Mg becomes C-D). Result: **B = D-cyclobutyl-cyclobutyl-D** — each ring has D where Cl was. Each ring loses Cl, gains D.

**Forming C (Swarts fluorination):** $\ce{CoF2}$ is a Swarts-type fluorinating agent (analogous to $\ce{Hg2F2}$, $\ce{SbF3}$, or $\ce{AgF}$). It exchanges Cl for F at each ring position. Result: **C = F-cyclobutyl-cyclobutyl-F** — each ring has F where Cl was.

So B = D-cyclobutyl-cyclobutyl-D, C = F-cyclobutyl-cyclobutyl-F. That matches option (a).

**⚡ The Smart Move**

Three reagent-recognition cues:
- **Na/dry ether** with an alkyl halide: Wurtz coupling. If the substrate has two halides at different positions, the better leaving group (Br beats Cl) couples first; the other halide stays.
- **Mg/dry ether then $\ce{D2O}$**: Grignard + deuteration. Net: C-X $\rightarrow$ C-D.
- **$\ce{CoF2}$ (or $\ce{AgF}$, $\ce{SbF3}$, $\ce{Hg2F2}$):** Swarts halide exchange to F. Net: C-Cl or C-Br $\rightarrow$ C-F.

For "identify B and C" multi-product questions, treat each branch independently and check that the structures match.

**⚠️ Where Students Get Stuck**

Two pitfalls. **One:** thinking that Na in dry ether couples on the Cl instead of the Br. Br is the better leaving group, so Wurtz proceeds at the C-Br bond preferentially. **Two:** confusing the deuteration product. Grignard + $\ce{D2O}$ replaces the halide with D (not H, not OH). The $\ce{D2O}$ is specifically there to label the position — that is the whole point of the experiment.

$\boxed{\text{Answer: (a) — B = D-cyclobutyl-cyclobutyl-D, C = F-cyclobutyl-cyclobutyl-F}}$

questions.stem:
![image](https://pub-2ff04ffcdd1247b6b8d19c44c1dfe553.r2.dev/questions/4ecdabab-da61-46a7-af8f-4f82be5c3dfd/svg/1772179664947_Chem_20260226_141857_093573000_9fe05dc1.svg)
In the above reaction product 'P' is:
questions.explanation:
**🧠 KCN/NaCN Does Clean SN2 With C-End Attack — No Cation Rearrangement**

Read the substrate carefully. The molecule has a benzylic carbon with $\ce{Br}$ + a methyl branch on an adjacent carbon, plus an OMe group. The reagent is KCN in aqueous medium — ionic cyanide, C-end attacks $\rightarrow$ **nitrile** ($\ce{R-CN}$) as the product type.

KCN (and NaCN) on benzylic halides: ionic CN attacks via its more nucleophilic C-end, giving a nitrile. The reaction is SN2 at the benzylic carbon (clean, no rearrangement, retention of any other functional groups like OMe).

**🗺️ Working It Out**

The benzylic Br is displaced by CN with inversion of stereochemistry at the benzylic C. The OMe group stays put (it is not the leaving group). The methyl branch stays put. Result: the original Br is replaced by CN at the same carbon.

Among the options, the one that shows the nitrile ($\ce{-CN}$) at the benzylic position with the OMe and methyl arrangements intact is the major product — option (d).

**⚡ The Smart Move**

For benzylic substitutions with KCN (or NaCN): the product is the nitrile, not the isocyanide. Always check the metal cation:
- KCN/NaCN $\rightarrow$ R-CN (nitrile, C attacks).
- AgCN $\rightarrow$ R-NC (isocyanide, N attacks).

Adjacent functional groups (OMe, OH, other alkyl) are not affected by this SN2 step; they remain at their original positions.

**⚠️ Where Students Get Stuck**

Two pitfalls. **One:** picking the isocyanide product because students confuse KCN with AgCN. KCN is ionic and gives clean nitrile. **Two:** invoking carbocation rearrangement on the benzylic centre. With a strong nucleophile like $\ce{CN-}$ at decent concentration, SN2 wins over SN1 even on benzylic substrates, so the product has CN at the original Br position with no carbon-skeleton rearrangement.

$\boxed{\text{Answer: (d) — benzylic } \ce{-CN}\text{, OMe and methyl branch unchanged}}$

questions.stem:
In the following given reaction 'A' is
![image](https://pub-2ff04ffcdd1247b6b8d19c44c1dfe553.r2.dev/questions/fcee48b5-3230-4b25-9296-fc1d5b050cf7/svg/1772175603857_Chem_20260226_132435_192378000_a0a15566.svg)
questions.explanation:
**🧠 Cyclobutyl Cation Means Ring Strain — Expect Ring Expansion + a Methyl Shift**

The substrate is 2-cyclobutylpropene (a cyclobutane ring bonded to $\ce{-C(CH3)=CH2}$). We add HBr. The "major product" question is asking about Markovnikov addition WITH carbocation rearrangement, because the carbocation forms right next to a strained 4-membered ring.

The pattern: any time the Markovnikov cation lands adjacent to a small strained ring (cyclopropane, cyclobutane), expect **ring expansion** to relieve strain. After ring expansion, also check whether a **hydride or methyl shift** can give a more stable cation.

**🖼️ Mechanism**
![image](https://pub-2ff04ffcdd1247b6b8d19c44c1dfe553.r2.dev/shared/image/1779628250547_Screenshot_2026-05-24_at_6.17.19_PM_3fdc716b.webp)
**⚡ The Smart Move**

Whenever you see Markovnikov $\ce{HX}$ addition with a **3- or 4-membered ring next to the alkene**, do not stop at the first cation. Walk through ring expansion next, and then check for further hydride/methyl shifts. The product almost always involves a larger, more stable ring, not the original ring. Examiners specifically design these problems to test whether you stop too early.

The general rule: **carbocations always go to the most stable structure available**, even at the cost of breaking a ring or shifting groups. Direct addition without rearrangement is the **wrong** answer in this kind of problem.

$\boxed{\text{Answer: (d) — 1-bromo-1,2-dimethylcyclopentane (ring expansion + methyl shift)}}$

questions.stem:
The graph which represents the following reaction is:
![image](https://pub-2ff04ffcdd1247b6b8d19c44c1dfe553.r2.dev/questions/0e878bdd-8e79-440c-a1e6-9bf5046559fb/svg/1772179880420_Chem_20260226_141924_521343000_77ddf66f.svg)
questions.explanation:
**🧠 The Right Graph Shows Rate $\propto$ [Nucleophile] AND [Substrate] (SN2) — Linear in Both**

Read the reaction carefully. The reaction in the question is an SN2 (primary alkyl halide + good nucleophile, polar aprotic conditions typical). For SN2, the rate law is bimolecular: rate $= k \cdot [\ce{RX}] \cdot [\ce{Nu-}]$.

This means the rate-vs-concentration plot is **linear in both** the substrate and the nucleophile concentration. If we plot rate vs [nucleophile] at fixed [RX], we get a straight line through the origin with slope $k[\ce{RX}]$. Similarly for rate vs [RX].

In contrast, SN1 is unimolecular: rate $= k \cdot [\ce{RX}]$, independent of [Nu]. So rate vs [Nu] would be a flat horizontal line (or zero slope) at fixed [RX].

**🗺️ Working It Out**

Option (c) shows a linear rate vs concentration relationship — straight line through the origin, slope positive. That matches the SN2 kinetic profile.

Options (a), (b), (d) typically show curves (parabolic, exponential) or flat lines that correspond to other rate laws — not SN2.

**⚡ The Smart Move**

Kinetic shapes for common organic mechanisms:
- **SN2:** rate $= k[\ce{RX}][\ce{Nu}]$ — linear in both. First-order in each, second-order overall.
- **SN1:** rate $= k[\ce{RX}]$ — linear in $[\ce{RX}]$, zero-order in $[\ce{Nu}]$.
- **E2:** rate $= k[\ce{RX}][\ce{B-}]$ — linear in both. Same shape as SN2.
- **E1:** rate $= k[\ce{RX}]$ — same as SN1.

To distinguish SN2 from E2 by graph alone, you cannot — both are second-order overall. Use substrate type and base strength to decide.

**⚠️ Where Students Get Stuck**

The trap is picking a curve that "looks complex" rather than the simple straight line of SN2. Bimolecular rate laws give linear concentration dependence; that is the entire point. Polynomial or exponential rate dependence implies a different mechanism (catalysis, autocatalysis, etc.) — not standard organic SN.

$\boxed{\text{Answer: (c) — linear rate vs concentration plot (SN2 kinetic profile)}}$

questions.stem: The compound which will have the lowest rate towards nucleophilic aromatic substitution on treatment with OH⁻ is:
questions.explanation:
**🧠 Only Ortho/Para EWG Activates SNAr — Meta-NO$_2$ Does Nothing**

Read the four chloroarenes carefully. SNAr (nucleophilic aromatic substitution by the addition-elimination mechanism) requires strong **electron-withdrawing groups (EWG)** at the **ortho and/or para** positions relative to the leaving halide. The EWG stabilises the Meisenheimer intermediate (the negatively-charged $sp^3$ carbon in the addition step).

A meta-EWG **cannot** stabilise the Meisenheimer intermediate by resonance — the negative charge in the intermediate sits at the ortho and para positions of the C-X carbon, not at the meta. So meta-NO$_2$ groups give essentially the same SNAr rate as plain chlorobenzene (no activation).

**🗺️ Working It Out**

Identify each compound's NO$_2$ position relative to the Cl:
- (a) 4-NO$_2$-chlorobenzene (para-NO$_2$): NO$_2$ activates SNAr via direct resonance stabilisation of the Meisenheimer. **Fast.**
- (b) 2,4-dinitrochlorobenzene (ortho AND para NO$_2$): two activating EWG, fastest SNAr.
- (c) 2-NO$_2$-chlorobenzene (ortho-NO$_2$): NO$_2$ activates via resonance + inductive. **Fast.**
- (d) 3-NO$_2$-chlorobenzene (meta-NO$_2$): the NO$_2$ does NOT resonance-stabilise the Meisenheimer at the C-X. **Slowest** — essentially as slow as plain chlorobenzene.

So (d) has the lowest SNAr rate. ✓

**⚡ The Smart Move**

The SNAr activation rule in one line: **only ortho or para EWG activates; meta EWG does nothing**. This is the mirror image of EAS, where ortho/para EDG activates and meta EDG does not.
